game_name: Zelda II: The Adventure of Link

danger_signals.visual_form: Small blob sprite (easy enemies) or large biped sprite (harder enemies) appearing randomly on map
danger_signals.meaning: Triggers transition to side-scrolling combat mode; larger/biped form indicates increased threat level

danger_signals.visual_form: Fortress-like structures with distinct palace graphics, window designs, and spike hazards
danger_signals.meaning: High-danger zones requiring progression through guarded puzzle areas

danger_signals.visual_form: Desert-dwelling enemies positioned in sand areas
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard indicator; originally placed too low to be visible (developer oversight)

danger_signals.visual_form: Specific enemy types with drain mechanics
danger_signals.meaning: Increased threat severity—defeats cause loss of accumulated experience points

safety_signals.visual_form: Fairy sprite appearing randomly during traversal
NPCs positioned in towns and safe zones
safety_signals.meaning: Interactive storytelling nodes; indicate safe areas and information hubs

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Link character (protagonist); Princess Zelda (sleeping, requires rescue)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Overworld map structure; towns with NPCs; palaces containing crystals and statues

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Generic enemy types (blobs, bipeds); palace guardians
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Small blob sprites (weaker minions) vs. large biped sprites (stronger minions)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Six guarded palaces scattered across Hyrule; desert regions with Leevers

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Post-Ganon era, Princess Zelda under sleeping spell
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Overworld map showing six distinct palaces as primary objectives; NPCs and signage reference the quest; introductory text explains the curse
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies spawn randomly during overworld traversal; palace encounters trigger side-scrolling combat sequences

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Crystals placed in statues; six palaces each have unique window graphics and architectural variations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Difficulty escalates with each palace cleared; enemy types intensify; experience requirements reset on save

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NES version: rapid rainbow color flashing screen (seizure risk); updated versions: red-shade flashing only
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game Over / Save Option Menu displayed; in Japanese FDS version, attribute levels reset to lowest of three if all lives lost (Western version permanent)

environmental_storytelling.example: Every palace has unique window designs; Maze Palace (4th) and Hidden Palace (6th) lack actual windows but retain window graphics (unused state)
Broken wall graphics exist but were never incorporated into palace design; indicates architectural storytelling framework left incomplete
Spike graphics exist for every palace but spikes never appear as actual hazards in final game; represents environmental design vocabulary that wasn't activated
Top-down overworld map serves as navigation layer; different terrain types (mountains, oceans) block or allow movement, encoding world geography as barrier/path system
Non-player characters in towns provide quest context; their presence marks safe zones contrasting with random enemy encounters in wilderness

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Player can selectively increase Attack, Magic, or Life attributes; choice deferred or committed based on accumulated experience. Western version: permanent levels; Japanese FDS version: resets to lowest attribute if all lives lost
Further experience in maxed attributes converts to extra lives instead of stat increase
Small blob icon = easy encounter; large biped icon = hard encounter; triggers transition to side-scrolling combat mode
Attack drains accumulated experience points (never causes permanent level loss, but delays progression)
Side-scrolling combat sequences; palace graphics and unique window designs encode location identity

notes: Zelda II uses a hybrid world-state communication system: top-down overworld map conveys strategic navigation and faction territories, while side-scrolling combat sequences communicate danger through enemy sprite size/type and health-drain mechanics. World state changes (post-Ganon, sleeping curse, crystal placement) are communicated through NPC dialogue and quest markers rather than visual transformation of existing environments. The game's experience system and lives mechanic create a separate layer of world-state communication tied to player progression status. Unused graphics (palace windows without corresponding hazards, spike tiles, broken walls) suggest an incomplete environmental storytelling vocabulary—these elements were visually prepared but mechanically deactivated, indicating design iteration at the asset level.
